title: Outdoor pig paddock rotation
subtitle: How to plan and manage a paddock system that prevents overgrazing and disease build-up.
excerpt: Pigs left in one paddock will turn it into bare compacted mud within weeks. A rotation system maintains some vegetation, reduces worm burden, and gives the land time to recover between occupancy.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork, outdoor pig management guide. Soil Association, organic pig production standards. NADIS, pig parasite management in outdoor systems.

content.text: A three or four-paddock rotation is the standard approach for outdoor pigs. One or two paddocks are occupied at any time; the others are resting and re-establishing vegetation. The rotation period depends on
and season; wet winters destroy vegetation faster than summer grazing.
Setting up paddocks with electric fencing

Pigs are contained reliably with two strands of electric fence. The lower strand should sit at approximately 20 cm from the ground, the upper at 40 to 50 cm. Use a mains energiser rated for the total fence length where possible; battery energisers are fine for small paddocks and temporary moves but require regular voltage checks. Electric fencing stakes can be pushed in by hand or with a post hammer for temporary divisions.
Training pigs to respect electric fencing

Pigs new to electric fencing need to be trained before they are relied upon to respect it. Put them into a small paddock with solid sides or stock netting outside the electric fence for the first few days; a pig that breaks through before it has had a few minor shocks will not learn quickly and may not stay in.
A minimum rest of 6 months between pig occupancies is the practical standard for reducing

on the ground. Ploughing or reseeding a rested paddock speeds vegetation recovery. Do not graze pigs on paddocks that were used for cattle or sheep in the same year without a rest period; cross-species parasite contamination is limited but some species are shared.

A pig board is useful for directing pigs through a gate rather than trying to herd them with your body. Pigs follow the edge of a solid barrier and can usually be guided through a gap by holding the board across the space you do not want them to go. Move to new ground when the existing paddock has been reduced to a heavily rooted, muddy state, not when it is completely destroyed.
